---
title: "Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information"
description: "Detailed study notes for Chapter 22 of Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications."
book_id: "evaluation-theory-models-applications"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Chapter"
chapter_number: 22
part: "Part Four: Evaluation Tasks, Procedures, and Tools"
source_pages: "555-579"
citation_key: "stufflebeam_coryn_2014_evaluation_theory_models_applications"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch22-chapter-22-collecting-evaluative-information:c0001"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch22-chapter-22-collecting-evaluative-information:c0002"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:key-standards-for-information-collection:c0040"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:key-standards-for-information-collection:c0045"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2014</span><span>Part Four: Evaluation Tasks, Procedures, and Tools</span><span>Chapter 22</span><span>Source pages 555-579</span></div>

This note summarises Chapter 22, "Collecting Evaluative Information," from Daniel L. Stufflebeam and Chris L. S. Coryn's *Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications* [@stufflebeam_coryn_2014_evaluation_theory_models_applications]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a substitute for the chapter.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source chunks at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the chapter's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, review questions, checklists, exhibits, or extended passages. When a checklist or table is central, the note describes its function and leaves the source artifact in the evidence record.

## Core Argument

Evaluative conclusions are only as defensible as the scope, quality, reliability, validity, and management of the information collected.

## Study Summary

Chapter 22 gives practical advice on information collection. It begins from standards because the credibility of answers depends on whether information is relevant, sufficient, reliable, valid, and responsibly managed.

The chapter proposes a broad information framework that can cover background, context, structure, operations, costs, outputs, outcomes, side effects, and stakeholder perspectives. The goal is to avoid collecting only what is easy or familiar.

Reliability and validity are treated as practical obligations. Evaluators should train collectors, document procedures, use multiple sources and methods when needed, check data quality, protect records, and report threats to validity.

The chapter also recognizes feasibility. Adequate scope must be balanced against burden, time, and cost. The evaluator's job is to gather enough credible information to answer priority questions and support justified conclusions.

For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluative information, information scope, reliability, and validity while moving through standards for collection, information collection framework, and sampling. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

## Key Concepts

- **evaluative information**: Information collected because it can support a judgment about merit, worth, significance, probity, or use.
- **information scope**: The range of context, inputs, activities, costs, outputs, outcomes, side effects, and perspectives considered.
- **reliability**: The consistency and dependability of measurement, coding, data collection, or judgment procedures.
- **validity**: The degree to which evidence and interpretations support the claims being made.
- **sampling**: The selection of cases, people, sites, records, or observations from which evaluation evidence will be collected.
- **information management**: Procedures for organizing, protecting, documenting, storing, retrieving, and controlling evaluation information.
- **multiple methods**: Use of more than one evidence source or method to strengthen coverage, triangulation, or credibility.
- **data quality**: The fitness of data for evaluation use, including relevance, completeness, reliability, validity, and traceability.

## Chapter Structure

- Standards for collection.
- Information collection framework.
- Sampling.
- Reliability and validity.
- Collection techniques.
- Information management.

## Practical Implications

- Plan information collection against each evaluation question and value criterion.
- Use multiple sources when single procedures cannot carry the claim.
- Document threats to validity and the steps taken to reduce them.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What value problem does Chapter 22 help evaluators handle?"]
  Central["Concept: Collecting Evaluative Information"]
  Concept1["Concept: evaluative information"]
  Concept2["Concept: information scope"]
  Mechanism1["Mechanism: standards for collection"]
  Mechanism2["Mechanism: information collection framework"]
  Implication1["Policy implication: Plan information collection against each evaluation question and value criterion."]
  Implication2["Policy implication: Use multiple sources when single procedures cannot carry the claim."]
  Question["Open question: What evidence would make this guidance credible in a live evaluation?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef evidence fill:#fff2df,stroke:#b45309,color:#7c2d12;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Chapter 21: Contracting Evaluations](chapter-21-contracting-evaluations.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter 23: Analyzing and Synthesizing Information](chapter-23-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What value problem does Chapter 22 help an evaluator diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before applying this chapter in a live evaluation?
3. How would this chapter change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, reporting, or use?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## Source Chunks

<details class="source-chunks">
<summary>Source chunks</summary>
<ul class="source-chunk-list">
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch22-chapter-22-collecting-evaluative-information:c0001</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch22-chapter-22-collecting-evaluative-information:c0002</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:key-standards-for-information-collection:c0040</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:key-standards-for-information-collection:c0045</code></li>
</ul>
</details>

## References

- Stufflebeam, D. L., & Coryn, C. L. S. (2014). *Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications*. Jossey-Bass. Chapter 22, pages 555-579.
- Saved retrieval metadata: `data/research-library/evidence/evaluation-theory-models-applications/chapter-evidence-summary.json`.
